metadata.summary: PM acts as integrator across all knowledge areas through single version-controlled integrated plan with cross-area impact analysis on every change and numeric threshold-driven status.

content.title: Project Integration Management
content.summary: The PM acts as integrator across all knowledge areas (scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, resources, communications, procurement) through a single version-controlled integrated plan. Every change request triggers cross-area impact analysis before approval; status colour is derived from numeric thresholds, not PM narrative; and closure is treated as integration's last mile — formal acceptance plus lessons learned plus archive.

why.p: Projects fail from silo management: scope changes approved without schedule updates, cost baselines drifting from schedule, risks without owners. Without integration, each area optimizes locally and the whole degrades. A machine-readable integrated plan with locked source-of-truth and automated threshold alerts catches cross-area drift before it compounds.

list.item: Programs with 2+ workstreams whose decisions interact (scope vs. schedule vs. cost vs. vendor).
Initiation phase: drafting the project charter to authorize work and bind sponsor commitment.
Change-heavy environments where every CR has cross-area impact simultaneously.
Multi-team or multi-vendor delivery where local optimization harms whole-system performance.
Project closure: final integration, lessons learned, formal acceptance, contract closeout.
Single-team, single-stream work with a stable backlog — Scrum or Kanban already integrates within the team.
Short tactical engagements under 4 weeks with no inter-area trade-offs — overhead exceeds value.
Pure research or discovery phases with no committed scope — wait until charter signal is real.
Solo-developer projects — keep artifacts lightweight (one-page charter, weekly check); the integrator is one person.

section.name: integration-role
section.p: The PM acts as the cross-area integrator: every decision in one knowledge area must be assessed for impact on all others before approval. Silo management optimizes parts and sub-optimizes the whole. A scope CR approved without schedule and cost update creates a multi-week cascade of drift that is invisible until delivery fails.

Integration coordinates: Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Risk, Resources, Communications, Procurement. Each has a subsidiary plan; the integrated plan references all of them from a single YAML source-of-truth.

rule.statement: The PM acts as the cross-area integrator: every decision in one knowledge area must be assessed for impact on all others before approval.
rule.rationale: Silo management optimizes parts and sub-optimizes the whole. A scope CR approved without schedule and cost update creates a multi-week cascade of drift that is invisible until delivery fails.

section.name: six-integration-activities
section.p: Core project integration management activities across the project lifecycle:

list.item: Project Charter (Initiation) — authorize project, define high-level scope, bind sponsor commitment. Charter as code: sponsor approves via PR review.
Project Plan (Planning) — integrate all subsidiary plans into one YAML referencing scope (WBS), schedule, cost, risks, quality, resources, comms, procurement baselines.
Direct Work (Execution) — lead execution; ensure the plan governs, not informal decisions.
Monitor and Control (Throughout) — track against integrated baselines; emit GREEN/YELLOW/RED from numeric thresholds, not PM narrative.
Integrated Change Control (Throughout) — every CR triggers cross-area impact analysis before approval; decisions logged in decisions/ per ADR pattern.
Close Project (Closure) — formal acceptance, lessons learned, archive, contract closeout. Closure is integration's last mile — do not let the team scatter before it completes.

section.name: charter-elements
section.p: Project charter as code defines authorization, high-level scope, success criteria, and sponsor commitment.

rule.statement: Every SMART objective in the charter must cite a measurable success criterion; unknowns must be listed as TO_CONFIRM, not omitted.
rule.rationale: Charter generators hallucinate constraints and assumptions to appear thorough. Forcing unknowns to surface as TO_CONFIRM creates a tracked open-question list.

section.name: integrated-change-control
section.p: Change control process that enforces cross-area impact analysis and decision logging.

rule.statement: Re-baseline only when scope/budget/contract changes — not when velocity slips. Re-baselining for slip masks chronic delivery problems.
rule.rationale: Baseline changes are decisions, not corrections. Treating slip as a reason to re-baseline hides the root cause and makes EVM meaningless.

rule.statement: Every approved CR requires a cross-area impact memo committed alongside the CR before implementation begins.
rule.rationale: Second-order effects (a 2-week delay shifting dependencies onto a holiday, breaking a vendor deadline) only surface when impact is computed across all areas — not as a delta on duration alone.

antipattern.description: Status colour declared GREEN/YELLOW/RED based on PM narrative rather than numeric threshold comparison.
antipattern.reason: Agents over-summarize: declaring "GREEN — on track" without numeric backing is the dominant integration failure. Colour must follow threshold arithmetic.

section.name: integrated-plan-as-code
section.p: Architecture for version-controlling the integrated project plan using YAML and git.

rule.statement: Single source of truth: one YAML file (integrated/plan.yaml) in the project repo; derivatives (slides, PDFs, dashboards) are generated — never hand-edited.
rule.rationale: Manual edits to derivative documents diverge from the source within one week; agents that pull from the wrong derivative produce misleading status reports.

section.p: Charter (charter.md) lives in the same repo; sponsor approves via PR merge. A decisions/ directory holds one ADR-style entry per approved CR. This makes the program's decision history searchable.

section.name: recommended-subagents
section.p: Specialized agents for different integration management tasks:

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drives integration setup as SDD tasks: TASK_charter, TASK_integrated_plan, TASK_change_control_process, TASK_status_dashboard, TASK_closure.
charter-author-agent (model: opus) — drafts charter from kickoff inputs, drives sponsor sign-off, captures assumptions and constraints.
change-impact-agent (model: sonnet) — given a CR, computes impact across scope/schedule/cost/quality/risk/resources from the integrated plan; emits a markdown impact memo.
baseline-curator-agent (model: sonnet) — keeps the integrated plan consistent — flags scope without schedule, schedule without cost, risks without owners.
status-reporter-agent (model: haiku) — renders the status report template from numeric inputs only — colour follows thresholds, never tone.
closure-agent (model: sonnet) — runs the closure checklist, archives plans, captures lessons learned, generates final acceptance documentation.
password-scrubber-agent — runs over charter/status/closure docs before distribution; integration artifacts are top leak paths.

section.name: change-impact-prompt-pattern
section.p: Strict markdown prompt pattern that forces numeric grounding and rejects vague recommendations:

example.code:
You are the change-impact agent. Inputs: 1. Approved change request (CR-NN.md): scope description, requestor, priority. 2. integrated/plan.yaml: scope (WBS), schedule, cost, risks, quality, resources. 3. Threshold table: GREEN/YELLOW/RED triggers per dimension.
Emit STRICT MARKDOWN: ## Impact Analysis CR-NN | Area | Current | Proposed | Delta | Status | Notes | |------|---------|----------|-------|--------|-------|
## Recommendation - decision: approve | reject | conditional - conditions: [ ... ] - new_baseline_required: true|false - communications: [ stakeholders to notify ]
Rules: - Numbers come from inputs; never invent. - Status follows threshold table verbatim. - Recommend reject if any dimension goes RED without a compensating trade-off. - Cite at least one risk-register entry if risk delta is non-zero.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when agents conduct integration management:

antipattern.description: Agent produces cross-area impact analysis that only covers the areas it has API access to.
antipattern.reason: An agent with only Jira access produces schedule-blind reports. Force an input audit: every knowledge area must appear in the impact memo or be explicitly marked "data unavailable".

antipattern.description: Closure declared complete with an empty lessons-learned file.
antipattern.reason: Agents skip lessons learned because there is no code change. Force the closure checklist to fail-close if the lessons file is empty.

antipattern.description: Charter sign-off automated by the agent merging its own PR.
antipattern.reason: Charter sign-off is a governance decision requiring sponsor authority; the agent prepares the PR and the sponsor approves the merge.

section.p: Mandatory human checkpoints: charter approval, baseline changes, CR approval, status colour shifts to RED, closure declaration.

Decision log entries get duplicated by parallel agents — force a unique-ID generator and advisory lock. Integrated plans over 500 lines blow context; page by knowledge area, persist summaries, rehydrate.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Command-line tools for integration management automation:

list.item: git + branch protection — charter and integrated plan as code; PR gates sponsor sign-off.
gh / glab — drive change-request issues, link to PRs that update baselines.
yq / jq — read/patch integrated/plan.yaml; aggregate dependencies.
python-docx / python-pptx / pandoc — render charter, status reports, closure docs to org-required formats.
mermaid-cli (mmdc) — render integration diagrams (scope → schedule → cost) and status dashboards.
mpxj — read/write MS Project .mpp schedules from the integrated plan.
taskjuggler / ganttproject-cli — compute critical path locally from YAML.
dvc / lakeFS — version control for non-code deliverables (CAD, datasets) referenced from the plan.
pre-commit — block edits to charter.md or baselines without rationale and sponsor reviewer.
make / just — standardize "build status pack" / "render closure" tasks.
Microsoft Project + Project for the Web — dominant predictive tool; integrates with Teams + Power BI for status.
Smartsheet — portfolio gantt + dashboards; common integration target.
Asana (Portfolios) / ClickUp (Goals) / Monday — lightweight portfolio with built-in status fields.
Jira (with Plans / Advanced Roadmaps) — common host; integrate via cross-project dashboards.
Azure DevOps — hybrid host: portfolio Plans + team Boards + Pipelines.
GitLab Premium/Ultimate (Roadmaps + Boards + Iterations) — single-pane integration alternative for engineering-heavy programs.
ServiceNow SPM (formerly ITBM) — heavy enterprise PPM with full integration; required where governance is the constraint.
Planview / Clarity / Sciforma — portfolio + EVM-grade tooling for large enterprises.
Confluence / Notion / Coda — charter, decisions, status home; pair with the work-graph host.
OpenProject / Redmine / Tuleap — self-hosted hosts for regulated/airgapped orgs.
Power BI / Tableau / Looker — status dashboards aggregating across tools.
Slack / MS Teams — status digest delivery; throttle to avoid noise.
DocuSign / Adobe Sign — charter sign-off, formal acceptance.
Box / SharePoint / Drive — repository for non-code deliverables; pair with dvc for versioning.

rule.statement: Single source of truth for the integrated plan (one YAML file, one repo). Derivatives (slides, docs, dashboards) are generated; never hand-edited.
rule.rationale: Manual edits diverge within one week; agents pulling from wrong derivatives produce misleading status.

rule.statement: Charter as code: charter.md lives in the project repo, sponsor approves via PR review with signed commit. Amendments require a new PR, not a stealth edit.
rule.rationale: Git history of charter changes is the audit trail for change authorization.

rule.statement: One decision log per project (decisions/), one ADR-style entry per integrated change. Search-first cultures depend on this.
rule.rationale: Decision rationale lost verbally; documented decisions become searchable corporate memory.

rule.statement: Status colour driven by numeric thresholds, never PM mood. Force the threshold table into the plan so the agent computes status, the human interprets it.
rule.rationale: Narrative status is subjective and unreliable; threshold-driven status is deterministic and auditable.

rule.statement: Every change request triggers cross-area impact analysis before approval; the impact memo is committed alongside the CR.
rule.rationale: Second-order effects only surface in cross-area analysis; single-area impact reviews hide integration risks.

rule.statement: Run an integration check weekly: scope without schedule? schedule without cost? risks without owners? — automate and open issues.
rule.rationale: Automated consistency checks catch silo drift in days rather than months.

rule.statement: For solopreneurs: keep the artifact lightweight (one-page charter, weekly integration check), but keep it. Skipping integration is the most common solopreneur failure.
rule.rationale: Even solo projects benefit from documented scope, schedule, risks, and decisions.

rule.statement: Closure is integration's last mile — formal acceptance + lessons learned + archive happens once, not after the team scatters.
rule.rationale: Delayed closure spreads knowledge loss and delays resource recovery.

rule.statement: Keep change-control velocity moderate — too few CRs hides chaos; too many drown the integrator. Aim for batched weekly review.
rule.rationale: Emergency CR streams bypass integrated impact analysis; batching forces discipline while preserving responsiveness.

rule.statement: Train sponsors on what they sign — most integration failures are sponsor-driven (silent scope additions).
rule.rationale: If sponsors do not understand the baseline they approved, every informal "can you just add X" becomes an untracked scope change.

list.item: "Integration" is the easiest dimension to skip on paper — every plan claims integration; few enforce it. Without a single source of truth, integration degrades to status theatre.
Charter rot: charters get signed and forgotten. If the charter does not change when scope/budget change, downstream teams use a stale "north star".
Change control without integration becomes whack-a-mole — scope CR approved, schedule and cost not updated, baselines drift apart.
Knowledge-area silos at the PMO produce inconsistent plans (schedule from MS Project, cost from Excel, risk in Confluence) that drift within weeks.
"Single source of truth" claims fail in practice when humans edit derivatives instead of the source — locked workflows are mandatory.
Solopreneurs treat integration as an afterthought; the lightweight section is correct but rarely practiced.
Status-report fatigue substitutes for actual integration decisions — colour codes without action are noise.

template.purpose: Project charter template with SMART objectives, success criteria, constraints, approval block
Weekly status report template with GREEN/YELLOW/RED per knowledge area

script.purpose: Script: compute status colours from numeric YAML inputs via threshold ladders
